text: The surgeon uses a hook electrocautery to divide extensive adhesions between the omentum and the gallbladder. The gallbladder infundibulum and cystic duct are traced to the choledochal cyst. The cystic duct is then clipped and divided using robotic endoshears.

The surgeon uses retraction and meticulous blunt and electrocautery dissection to circumferentially separate the choledochal cyst from adhesions to the duodenum and surrounding soft tissues. The cyst is periodically decompressed by removing and replacing the cystic duct clip, and dissection continues laterally, medially, and posteriorly until the cyst is completely mobilized.
The surgeon carefully dissects the distal portion of the choledochal cyst, which is abutting the portal vein, using precise techniques to avoid damage to the adjacent vascular structure.
A circumferential umbilical tape is placed around the surgical site to maintain retraction during the procedure.

The surgeon continues distal dissection of the cyst, observing a sharp tapering to approximately two to three millimeters near the pancreatic head. The common bile duct is ligated using 3-0 PDS and 3-0 Vicryl sutures, followed by division after placing a proximal clip.

The surgeon applies traction on the cystic duct stump and manipulates the distal portion of the cyst, dissecting it completely from the proximal portal vein towards the porta hepatis. The first and proximal second portions of the duodenum are mobilized, revealing the anatomical structures involved.

The surgeon divides the cyst half a centimeter distal from the confluence of the right and left hepatic ducts. Subsequently, a one-centimeter duodenotomy is created five centimeters distal to the pylorus in an anti-mesenteric longitudinal fashion.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn interrupted hepatico-duodenostomy, using three ovicle sutures. Four stitches are placed on the posterior wall and six stitches on the anterior wall, connecting the hepatic duct to the duodenum.
The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy and places a drain at the anastomotic site, ensuring proper drainage post-procedure.
